speaker: interviewee
text: Handa Stark.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were back in your village.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were back in our village, grandmother, grandfather, chil-- you know, cousins, everybody. Everybody was home. Things were not the same as they were before, but everybody was home, and we were healthy, and we were glad for that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In '43, it was-- it was at [INAUDIBLE] time. That's after [? Paisach ?] so it's-- it's-- it was May, I think, or close to that. That's when the-- the ghetto started. I think at [INAUDIBLE]. They-- they left us out because they needed my father. But the rest of the people, they had to go in the ghetto. And we slept two nights in the ghetto also. They they le-- Then they brought us out. And in the ghetto, it was really terrible. Because families where, like, if somebody had two bedrooms and a kitchen and a living room, then five families were put in there. And there was really nothing to do and the sanitation was very bad. People-- It wasn't-- it wasn't like-- like it is here, plenty of water, and everything else. Now, the people from the ghetto, they were taken to do all kind of demeaning things. The-- the people had to wash the sidewalks on their knees with rags. They had to sweep-- the-- the doctors and lawyers-- they had to sweep the streets. The-- they were really just being mean, and just-- just demeaning. Now when you went into the ghetto, you had to go through a search. They searched you everywhere. They were worried that you are taking in diamonds or-- or I don't know what. Now, this is why when, finally they came-- when the transports started or the people started to be taken away-- they came for us, and they said to my mother that, you know, Mrs. Josipovis, take everything valuable with you. And the-- and my mother told the gendarme and the man who-- the civilian who came-- says, listen, mister. If they are taking us to be butchered, I don't need no jewelry or gold. And if I will survive this and I come back, I'm not worried that I'm not going to have gold and jewelry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we didn't take anything. Nothing. I-- I had Keds, like what they wear here, Keds? They're tennis shoes. That's what I ran away. We didn't take nothing. There were people that were schlepping suitcases, heavy, you know. I don't know what was in it. And then they were taking us to the-- so I shouldn't jump ahead. They took us to the-- to the synagogue, to the big syn-- main synagogue. And there, everybody was searched. And when they were taking you away, you were searched also. And my mother was very upset, and we looked at each other. And I saw a few girlfriends that are going away, and I told my mother, Mother, why go through all this again? Let's go now, and we don't have to go through this again. And mother said, you're right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we told the people that we want to go with this transport. And they lined us up, and we had to walk to the train station, which was about, like, 10 blocks away. But there were older people, people who had no children, and they had to do everything for themselves. And they were carrying these heavy suitcases, or some people had wicker baskets full with stuff. And the policemen with the-- with the whip, they were just beating them and-- and driving them. And I had nothing, so I helped a little. And finally, we came to the train station, and everybody was loaded in train cars. Here, the most terrible experience was again, because you were with the-- I was-- you can cling to your mother or father, so you feel-- you don't feel so bad. There was no facility to go to the bathroom. There was a pot, and everybody had to go on that. Now, this is, I don't know. This is such a dehumanizing [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] something, when there is 4-- there are 40 people. You are on top of each other, and you have to use this pot, and it sloshes, and it-- it's terrible. Anyway, we had a little food. My father didn't want to eat. Then we had to tell that my mother, Daddy, this is getting spoiled already, so eat it. You know. So it remained-- when we came back and we were [INAUDIBLE], remained the joke that we have to tell daddy that this is getting spoiled, he should eat. And my father was very worried because we already knew that how these Germans are and what's happening in Poland, when we heard that they were killing the mothers with their babies in their arms, and-- and throwing people alive into the river to drown, tied them together and they drowned and atro-- atroc-- atrocities like that. We didn't expect anything good from this to come out. But still, there was a ray of hope that they will take us and we will work. The old people, they will take care of the children, or cooking, and washing, and the young people will work. And my mother always said, I wouldn't like to do that. I would like to work. My father, he was very worried that they were going to take the young women to the soldiers to the front. And he had enough, be-- before we went to the s-- to the synagogue, he stopped in the pharmacy and he got poison that if he w-- and he had a syringe. That he had an idea that we should kill ourselves. That we shouldn't go through this, because nothing is, you know, and they're gonna-- we should kill ourselves. And when he told us, I told Daddy-- and I don't know where I took the courage, because my-- my mother and father, they're very, very strict people-- and I told my-- my father, Daddy, I'm so young. I never even loved, or was loved by somebody. I don't want to die. And don't worry, if they taking-- they will take us to the-- for something like that, there's always a way that I can kill myself. And so he didn't do it. And then the train finally, after three days, stopped. Then they opened the doors and there were the Germans with the German shepherds and-- dogs-- and there were some inmates in striped uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was Auschwitz. And they told us that, first of all, to separate men from women, and line up. And there was Mengele. This is a man-- very handsome, dark man-- with a big, black cape, with a-- with a crop. With a crop. With black boots, shiny boots. And just with the swish of the crop, he was separating the-- that somebody went to the right, and somebody went to the left. And there were no questions asked. These men in the uniforms, when they saw a-- a woman-- a young woman with a child-- then they would say, give the-- give the baby to the mother. Give the baby to the mother. And because people had this idea that the young will work and the older will take care, many of them did. Many didn't want to, but many of them did. And then we were like in the beginning of the-- the line. And they sent my mother to the one side, and they send me to the other side. And you couldn't say nothing, or you couldn't-- nobody asked you anything. And this went on for a little bit, just very little bit. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother, where was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in forced labor camp. He wasn't with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was, but we were right away separated the men from the women. And he noticed that he made a mistake and he wanted that the ones who he sent to the right should go to the left, and the ones who he sent to the left should go to the right. And he said, umtauschen. Change. And when we were changing, we grabbed each other, mother and I. And because she wanted to go to work, and she felt I'm going to work, she came with me. And this was the biggest miracle, because otherwise she would not have survived. Now, they took us from here. It was a short distance to a place where they-- they cut our hair. First they told us-- I don't remember whether first we got undressed or first they cut our hair. First I think we got undressed. We could only keep our shoes. And they cut our hair. Some, they had the shears. Some, they had just scissors. And you right away looked that-- that you almost couldn't recognize each other. They took all our clothes and they sent us to a place where there was a shower, but it was really just-- you know, there was hardly any water coming out of it. And after that, they pushed us. They made us go to another room and stand there. Now you were standing, standing in line, or in rows, and I got ver-- or my mother or I, we got very thirsty. So I said, I'm going to go and look for some water. As I step out of the line, somebody says, [INAUDIBLE]. I-- a cousin. And then-- so anyway, we were there, four cousins that we were standing there already over an hour and we didn't even know we are there. And there was no water. And then they gave everybody a dress. They didn't look that, you know, you put on this dress. The short one got a long one, a long one got a short one. It-- it-- you couldn't switch or you couldn't say, this doesn't fit me. You just put it on, and you kept quiet. Then, li-- they made you line up. It was very muddy. They made you line up and you went again. And then you-- they took you in a-- there it happened that I was not in the same row with my mother. I got-- because they counted five, and then you go in the other row. And I wanted to tell the woman-- and it was not a German. It was an-- a Pol-- a woman-- that I want to go there because my mother is there. And she gave me such a slap, and she pushed me back in the other row. Now I always felt that she was cruel. But later, I thought about it, and I think that she was being kind to me. Because she was there already many years and she saw, and she knew, that if the Germans will know-- will know that there is a mother and daughter, you know, they may all take the mother out, or separate us totally. And she wanted I should keep quiet that that is my mother. Anyway, they took us to a barrack. There were bunk beds. Three. Three-- I mean, two, one on-- no, three. One, two, three. And there were 10 girls to a bunk. There was one horse blanket on the bottom, and there was one to cover ourselves. And they-- in the early in the morning, they brought-- yeah. And when we got there, the-- the Aufseherin-- that was the-- who took care on this group of people, and I think that there were about 300 or more in one bunk-- in one building. She told-- She t-- and the way the building was constructed, in the middle ran like a-- a-- it was supposed to be an oven to heat the barrack. There was never any fire in it. But like in the begin-- in one end, there was like a chimney and where you could put in the fire. And then this ran, made out of bricks, like-- how would we call it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was about this wide and this tall, and it ran the length of the-- of the-- the buil-- the-- the barrack.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like a brazier. It was like a brazier.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But it was-- there was never any fire in it. But these women who wanted to talk to us who were in the-- they would stand up on it and then walk on it, and they could see everybody better. They told us that [INAUDIBLE] the Germans will come in and they will ask you, does your head hurt? Is anybody who has a sore throat? Anybody has a pimple? Don't you ever, ever say anybody, that you have something like that. Because this is just an excuse. They going to take you away and you will never come back. So then, in the morning, they fed us a little black coffee. Early, early in the morning, like maybe 4 o'clock in the morning. After that you had to go out of this barrack and stand, five abreast in rows, because you were counted. And they also wanted that the barrack, they were very clean. You know, they wanted to be very-- keep us very healthy, that the barracks should air out. Now, after this they took us to the bathroom. And you could-- there was a latrine, and on the other side, there was-- there were-- there was water. There was a-- like a-- like a-- a trough, you know, ran the length. And you could open the water. And they told us not to drink the water because we're going to get diarrhea. And-- but some people drank it anyway because you were so thirsty that-- that you didn't care. And after that, we came back in the barracks, and we just had to-- we just had to lay there, do nothing. Then, at lunch time, they brought some kind of vegetables cooked together with some barley or something. But it was cooked I don't know what time, and it was in big containers, like milk containers. And then you opened it. It was kind of bubbling, like it's getting fermented. It's-- it's-- it was awful. It was grey and smelly, and-- and the worst part was that each 10 people, they had one bowl. And they put this gruel in this bowl, and you had to pass it around. And everybody-- there was no spoons. Everybody slobbered into it. I could never eat it. They told me I'm going to die, but I just couldn't eat it. And in the evening, then, after that, we went out again in the a-- eve-- about four o'clock, and we have to stand in rows again, and we were counted. And when we came back, then everybody got a little piece of bread. And one day it was a little marmalade, one day it was a slice like liverwurst, one day it was a little patch of margarine. And this was what we lived on. I-- I never ate. Not the-- the breakfast. Sometimes for breakfast they had like [? Farina. ?] But it-- I don't know. They maybe sweetened it with saccharin or something. It was terribly sweet and-- and bad. So this bread and this little something kept us going. Now, this-- from this barrack, they took us over to a place that was called a [INAUDIBLE]. This housed about 32,000 people. It was a long, long area, barracks on both sides. As you came in, the Germans had their-- a little house for themselves. And on the two sides were the kitchens, two kitchens. And then the barracks went like this. In the middle, there was on one side a latrine, on the other side the-- the wash room. And one barrack was like an infirmary. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Work, we didn't do. We just had to lay on this thing, on this-- these beds. And we were counted, and we were selected from time to time. They-- they made you take off your clothes, and if they saw that, you know, women have kind of stretch marks, or stretched out stomachs, indicating that they already had babies or so, without any question they were led away, and we didn't know where. [INAUDIBLE] and these Slovak and Polish girls who were there already many years, they used to tell us, because the Germans told some people they're gonna-- there is a selection for work. And these Polish girls told us that, don't believe that, because there is no work. The only way you go out from here is up. And then we saw the belching of the smoke and the terrible stench, and we put two and two together. That maybe there is more to it that-- than we know. And that especially-- and that-- that these-- these Aufseherin, what they were called, the ones who-- Aufseherin is actually looking over.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the-- the barrack, the ones that they liked, they tried to keep that they shouldn't go away. And they liked me also, because they liked my mother. And we-- we stayed in Auschwitz for six months. And I really was lucky, because I was never hit. I was never-- I never saw a dead person while I was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard, but I didn't go to look, that there were some, mainly mothers, who lost babies. And they would scream at night. And it was terrible. And sometimes they just went out and grabbed onto the electrical wires, and they were electrocuted. And I really, now that I'm a mother, I still-- I-- I just can't believe that how really they survived. When-- when babies were taken out of their hands, and they knew that, after a while, that they were-- they were killed. Anyhow, they started to take, for-- for-- they started to take for work. And we were there already six months, and my cousins were very anxious to leave because they-- this was-- the crematorium was going constantly, and it was very dangerous.

speaker: interviewer
text: Weren't you frightened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wasn't I frightened? Yes. But I-- I was not frightened for myself. I was frightened for my mother. Because see, this was-- in this-- we were separated three times, on three different occasions. And through, really, God's help, I always smuggled myself back to my mother. On one instance, when I-- I tried to beat my way out of it, you know, and the German just pushed me back. And they brought me-- because what they did, that they would empty a barrack and then they would select the people, bring it in the barrack that's empty, and take them away. And then people could come back there again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it just so happened that this selection was-- the people was-- were brought back to number three, the barrack three, where we were. And then the Aufseherin who was there-- the block [INAUDIBLE]. I'm using the wrong word. The block [INAUDIBLE], there was, not the Aufseherin. The block [INAUDIBLE]. That was the name of the person who was the head of the-- the block that was the barrack. OK? Correction. So she says, what are you doing here? Where is your mother? And I said, they separated me. And she said, pretend like you are working here. And then the Germans will open the front door, and say, 'Raus'. Then you go out the back door. And that's what I did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came back to my mother, and she was sitting on the top bunk, just in a position like this, you know, like her whole life has ended. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [CRYING] And then she saw me, so we hugged and we were together again. So when--

speaker: interviewer
text: Another miracle.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when-- when it was the selections and they didn't want to take my mother, I always stayed behind, and my cousins also. And one day, two Germans came and [INAUDIBLE] civilians. And they said to the group that they are going to take 500 women to a place where there was never bombed. They will work in a factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everybody will have their own bed, and everybody will have their own blanket, and everybody will have their own cup. And we thought, th-- this is heaven. And we lined up, and then my mother was in the-- and-- and so the Germ-- the-- the civilians were standing here, and we were lined up here on one side of this whatever we call it, you know. The middle of the-- what did you say you called it? It was like a brazier?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a brazier. We were on this side, and we had to go around and come in front of the two civilians who will select. And an SS was standing here to direct traffic. And each time my mother approached, he pushed her away. And he says, du bist schon zu alt. You are too old. And all my cousins were taken. And my mother was a courageous person, and she was standing here, and the civilians were standing here. My mother spoke fluently German. And she took the courage and she says dear sirs, I was-- and the German, the SS, wants to push my mother away. And the-- the civilians, who was a good natured person, said, let her talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother said that I was just a young girl, I was in America, and I worked for Ford Motor Company, which she did. And they asked her what did she do, and how long did she do it. And they told this German, you see? If nobody comes out of the 500, but we want this woman to come. OK? And this was another miracle.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we all went. They took us first to the bath. And in the bath, they again, they looked us over. And they made my mother a cross on her chest. Because we were all naked, and I don't know what they saw, or what they didn't see, and they made a cross.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I had a very clever cousin-- a young cousin-- and she said, what do you have there? And she took something, and she rubbed it off. And so we all got clothes and we slept over in the barrack. And the next day we were put on a train-- again on cattle cars-- and we were on our way.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we arrived to a place that's called Ruzomberok. That is in the Sudetenland. And then we got off the train, and we had to walk about four kilometers. It was winter, and it was cold, but it was not unbearable. And really, they took us into a beautiful building, and everybody got a bed. There were just two bunks, one on top of the other. And everybody got two blankets, and a cup. And the next day, they brought us the most delicious food, with cauliflower and peas and carrots. And we thought, heaven. Well, that didn't last too long because they were not set up for the kitchen and they brought us from the German officers' kitchen the food, and that's why it was so delicious. But we stayed here six months. And we just had to walk across the railroad tracks, and the factory was there. And we worked on the V2s, V two, it was called. This was a missile-- a missile. And two girls, or one girl, they-- we all worked with Germans. Some were SS and some were civilian. And there were two shifts. And the food was absolutely terrible. We were starving, we were so hungry. The bread they only gave us when it was moldy. The soup was so-- such a liquid. Just water. And we were very hungry. But the work was not hard, and the ro-- the house was very warm. And we had lots of hot water that we could wash ourselves, and we could clean ourselves so we were very-- we were never sick. Nobody was sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I developed a-- a vitamin deficiency. I had spots on my-- you know, I could pull my-- my skin would come off. And in my mouth, I had sores. On my neck. And my mother took the liberty-- the Germans, they were popping vitamins in their mouth all the time. So she asked that, could you give some to my daughter? She-- she says, no. We have enough for ourselves, but not for you Jews. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, we saw that they starting to evacuate the factory. And we had to take things out on the train and put everything on the trains. One night, they needed volunteers. And-- and I was always ready and I said, I'll go. I had no shoes. Just wooden-- not even clogs, just-- just the sole and there was just this strip of material on top.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I go volunteer. And with the girls, we were taking this stuff off and singing, just singing. All kind of songs. Hungarian song, German songs, Russian songs, Czech songs. And as I'm going by with two other girls, and the-- from the train, from the-- from the locomotive or the-- a man says, do you speak Czech?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, yes. And he says, wait a minute. And he gives me a whole loaf of bread. Now this, you have to understand, that we're taught that if they evacuate the factory, after that, they're going to evacuate us. We will have to go. Not only that we didn't have any shoes, but we were starved. And my mother-- once my mother said, go and try to get something to eat. So I go to the kitchen and I asked the-- the person who worked there, give me a little potato peels. I didn't ask for the potato, just some potato peels. And she didn't want to give me. Then-- then I broke down. I cried so hard that I can't bring my mother even-- and I knew the woman from our hometown. That she would be so cruel that she wouldn't give me even some potato peels. Anyway, when I saw this bread, I thought, we are saved. And I brought the bread to my mother, and we put it away. We never ate it. Because we were-- we were keeping it, that when they were taking-- if they will take us, then we should have it for the trip. But the Russians came sooner. And even though the-- the Germans told us that, don't be so happy because we have the possibility to kill you just like that in two minutes. And you're not going to survive. But by the time the bombs started to come real close, you know, they were so worried about themselves that they were running around like chickens without heads, trying to get little carts and push carts and go someplace. And they left us alone. And May, I think it was 15, or the fifth, I don't remember exactly, we were liberated by the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was--
